analogy: Think of "Carbon and Its Compounds" through the chapter's own opening images: Science 58 Carbon and its Compounds 4 CHAPTER In the last Chapter, we came to know many compounds of importance to us. In this Chapter we will study about some more interesting compounds and their properties. Also, we shall be learning about carbon, an element... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 4 CHAPTER, 4.1 BONDING IN CARBON – THE COVALENT BOND, Q U E S T I O N S, 4.2 VERSATILE NATURE OF CARBON. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Carbon and Its Compounds is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that science 58 Carbon and its Compounds 4 CHAPTER In the last Chapter, we came to know many compounds of importance to us.

The text explains that in this Chapter we will study about some more interesting compounds and their properties.

The text explains that also, we shall be learning about carbon, an element which is of immense significance to us in both its elemental form and in the combined form.

The text explains that things made Things made Others of metal of glass/clay Activity 4.1 Activity 4.1 Activity 4.1 Activity 4.1 Activity 4.1 n Make a list of ten things you have used or consumed since the morning. n Compile this list with the lists made by your classmates and then sort the items into the adjacent Table. n If there are items which are made up of more than one material, put them into both the relevant columns of the table.

The text explains that look at the items that come in the last column of the above table filled by you – your teacher will be able to tell you that most of them are made up of compounds of carbon.

The text explains that what would be the product if a compound containing carbon is burnt?

The text explains that food, clothes, medicines, books, or many of the things that you listed are all based on this versatile element carbon.

The text explains that in addition, all living structures are carbon based.

The text explains that the amount of carbon present in the earth’s crust and in the atmosphere is quite meagre.

The text explains that the earth’s crust has only 0.02% carbon in the form of minerals (like carbonates, hydrogen- carbonates, coal and petroleum) and the atmosphere has 0.03% of carbon dioxide.

The text explains that in spite of this small amount of carbon available in nature, the importance of carbon seems to be immense.

The text explains that in this Chapter, we will know about the properties of carbon which make carbon so important to us.

The text explains that 4.1 BONDING IN CARBON – THE COVALENT BOND In the previous Chapter, we have studied the properties of ionic compounds.

The text explains that we saw that ionic compounds have high melting and boiling points and conduct electricity in solution or in the molten state.

The text explains that carbon and its Compounds 59 saw how the nature of bonding in ionic compounds explains these properties.

The text explains that let us now study the properties of some carbon compounds.

The text explains that most carbon compounds are poor conductors of electricity as we have seen in Chapter 2.

The text explains that from the data given in Table 4.1 on the boiling and melting points of the carbon compounds, we find that these compounds have low melting and boiling points as compared to ionic compounds (Chapter 3).

The text explains that we know that the reactivity of elements is explained as their tendency to attain a completely filled outer shell, that is, attain noble gas configuration.

The text explains that can you draw the electron dot structure for this molecule?

The text explains that so each atom of oxygen shares two electrons with another atom of oxygen to give us the structure shown in Fig.

The text explains that the electron dot structure of N2 and its triple bond can be depicted as in Fig.

The text explains that can you draw the electron dot structure for this molecule showing how all four atoms achieve noble gas configuration?

The text explains that carbon and its Compounds 61 These two different structures result in diamond and graphite having very different physical properties even though their chemical properties are the same.

The text explains that graphite is also a very good conductor of electricity unlike other non-metals that you studied in the previous Chapter.

The text explains that diamonds can be synthesised by subjecting pure carbon to very high pressure and temperature.

The text explains that the structure of diamond The structure of C-60 Buckminsterfullerene Q U E S T I O N S ?

The text explains that what would be the electron dot structure of carbon dioxide which has the formula CO2?

The text explains that what would be the electron dot structure of a molecule of sulphur which is made up of eight atoms of sulphur?

The text explains that (Hint – The eight atoms of sulphur are joined together in the form of a ring.) Figure 4.5 Figure 4.5 Figure 4.5 Figure 4.5 Figure 4.5 Electron dot structure for methane points of these compounds.

The text explains that in diamond, each carbon atom is bonded to four other carbon atoms forming a rigid three-dimensional structure.

The text explains that graphite structure is formed by the hexagonal arrays being placed in layers one above the other.

The text explains that we have also seen the structure of a simple carbon compound, methane.

The text explains that in the beginning of the Chapter, we saw how many things we use contain carbon.

The text explains that one reason for the formation of strong bonds by carbon is its small size.

The text explains that carbon and its Compounds 63 Organic compounds The two characteristic features seen in carbon, that is, tetravalency and catenation, put together give rise to a large number of compounds.

The text explains that these compounds were initially extracted from natural substances and it was thought that these carbon compounds or organic compounds could only be formed within a living system.

The text explains that 4.2.1 Saturated and Unsaturated Carbon Compounds 4.2.1 Saturated and Unsaturated Carbon Compounds 4.2.1 Saturated and Unsaturated Carbon Compounds 4.2.1 Saturated and Unsaturated Carbon Compounds 4.2.1 Saturated and Unsaturated Carbon Compounds We have already seen the structure of methane.

The text explains that in order to arrive at the structure of simple carbon compounds, the first step is to link the carbon atoms together with a single bond (Fig.

The text explains that for example, the structure of ethane is arrived in the following steps – C—C Step 1 Figure 4.6 Figure 4.6 Figure 4.6 Figure 4.6 Figure 4.6 (a) Carbon atoms linked together with a single bond Three valencies of each carbon atom remain unsatisfied, so each is bonded to three hydrogen atoms giving: Step 2 Figure 4.6 Figure 4.6 Figure 4.6 Figure 4.6 Figure 4.6 (b) Each carbon atom bonded to three hydrogen atoms The electron dot structure of ethane is shown in Fig.

The text explains that can you draw the structure of propane, which has the molecular formula C3H8 in a similar manner?

The text explains that figure 4.6 Figure 4.6 Figure 4.6 Figure 4.6 Figure 4.6 (c) Electron dot structure of ethane More to Know!

The text explains that science 64 The electron dot structure for ethene is given in Fig.

The text explains that can you draw the electron dot structure for ethyne?

The text explains that explain the formation of scum when hard water is treated with soap.

The text explains that what change will you observe if you test soap with litmus paper (red and blue)?

The text explains that which of the following hydrocarbons undergo addition reactions: C2H6, C3H8, C3H6, C2H2 and CH4.

The text explains that give a test that can be used to differentiate between saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons.

The text explains that explain the mechanism of the cleaning action of soaps.

The text explains that i Use molecular model kits to make models of the compounds you have learnt in this Chapter.

The text explains that iI n Take about 20 mL of castor oil/cotton seed oil/linseed oil/soyabean oil in a beaker.

The text explains that heat the mixture with continuous stirring for a few minutes till the mixture thickens.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Science, Chapter 4: Carbon and Its Compounds
